findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/workflow/test_plan_review_workflow.py` as an explicit edit target (or require a backward-compat re-export of the three sink functions in `plan_review/orchestrator.py`) to satisfy AC3's 'existing test suites continue to pass unmodified' promise.
evidence: Remediation says: 'Update plan_review/orchestrator.py's and plan_review/workflow_ops.py's existing call sites to import it from its new home'
AC3 says: 'Existing plan-review and code-review test suites continue to pass unmodified (the sink move is a mechanical relocation)'

tests/unit/workflow/test_plan_review_workflow.py line 582: `from rebar.llm.plan_review import orchestrator` then directly calls `orchestrator.collect_contract_violations()`, `orchestrator.record_contract_violation(...)`, `orchestrator.drain_contract_violations()` — these will fail if the symbols move without a re-export shim
findings.finding: tests/unit/workflow/test_plan_review_workflow.py directly imports all three sink functions (collect_contract_violations, record_contract_violation, drain_contract_violations) from rebar.llm.plan_review.orchestrator (lines 582-634), but this file is not listed as an edit target in the Remediation or AC3. Moving the sink to review_kernel without either updating these imports or adding a backward-compat re-export in orchestrator.py will break this test suite, violating AC3's promise that 'existing test suites continue to pass unmodified'.
findings.location: Remediation §1 / AC3 / tests/unit/workflow/test_plan_review_workflow.py:582-634
scenarios: When the sink moves to review_kernel/verify.py or review_kernel/telemetry.py and orchestrator.py no longer defines the three symbols, `pytest tests/unit/workflow/test_plan_review_workflow.py -q` fails with AttributeError.
AC3's 'unmodified' promise is violated unless the plan explicitly requires a backward-compat shim or lists this test file as an edit target.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a re-export shim in plan_review/orchestrator.py after moving the sink: `from rebar.llm.review_kernel.telemetry import collect_contract_violations, record_contract_violation, drain_contract_violations` (or equivalent). This keeps all existing importers working without any test modifications, which matches AC3's 'mechanical relocation / byte-identical behavior' framing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete YAML workflow-binding changes required to thread `outcome_counts` from `code_review_decide`'s output into `code_review_coach`'s coverage (i.e., add a `coverage`/`outcome_counts` `with:` entry to the verdict step in `code-review.yaml` and specify how `code_review_coach` in `workflow_ops.py` should read and propagate it), mirroring the exact hierarchy_incomplete threading specification.
evidence: Plan: 'Thread it into each pipeline's verdict coverage the same way `hierarchy_incomplete` was threaded in ticket b24d: `coverage["outcome_counts"]` set by `*_decide`'s output.'
code-review.yaml verdict step (lines 178-186): wires only blocking/surfaced/dropped/indeterminate/notes — no coverage passthrough from decide.
code_review_coach (workflow_ops.py ~line 502): `coverage = ctx.inputs.get('coverage') or {'llm_ran': True}` — only reads coverage if passed as an input.

plan-review.yaml coach step passes hierarchy_incomplete explicitly from precheck, not from decide — but outcome_counts would live on decide's output, requiring new YAML bindings that the plan does not enumerate.
ABSENCE: no YAML binding specification for outcome_counts in the plan's remediation steps.
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `outcome_counts` flows from `*_decide`'s output dict into `coverage["outcome_counts"]` at verdict-assembly time, mirroring the `hierarchy_incomplete` threading pattern — but omits the concrete YAML workflow binding changes (the `with:` entries in plan-review.yaml and code-review.yaml) needed to carry `outcome_counts` from the `decide` step into the `coach`/`verdict` step. For code-review this gap is acute: `code-review.yaml`'s `verdict` step (code_review_coach) currently receives only `blocking`, `surfaced`, `dropped`, `indeterminate`, `notes` — there is no `coverage` passthrough from `decide` — and `code_review_coach` in workflow_ops.py reads coverage from `ctx.inputs.get('coverage') or {'llm_ran': True}`, so an unspecified wiring leaves `outcome_counts` silently absent from the verdict coverage even if `code_review_decide` returns it.
findings.location: Remediation section, step 2 ('outcome_counts at the *_decide boundary') and AC line 1; src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/code-review.yaml verdict step; src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py code_review_coach
scenarios: Implementer adds `outcome_counts` to `code_review_decide`'s return dict but never wires it into code-review.yaml's verdict step → `coverage['outcome_counts']` is silently missing from the final verdict, and AC1 is not satisfied.
Implementer wires plan-review.yaml correctly but misses code-review.yaml (no coverage passthrough exists there), creating the exact asymmetry the ticket is meant to close.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Remediation step 2, add: 'In `code-review.yaml`, add `outcome_counts: ${{ steps.decide.outputs.outcome_counts }}` to the `verdict` step's `with:` block; in `code_review_coach` (workflow_ops.py), read `ctx.inputs.get("outcome_counts")` and set `coverage["outcome_counts"] = outcome_counts` when present. In `plan-review.yaml`, add `outcome_counts: ${{ steps.decide.outputs.outcome_counts }}` to each `coach` step's `with:` block, and read it in `plan_review_coach` alongside `hierarchy_incomplete`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `collect_contract_violations()` in `_run_code_review_gate` must wrap the FULL function body (including the `finalize_code_review_verdict` call at line 644, outside `gate_read_root`), not just `_ex.run_workflow`, so the ContextVar sink is still active when `finalize_code_review_verdict` drains it.
evidence: Plan says: 'activate collect_contract_violations() around the workflow run in gate_dispatch._run_code_review_gate (mirroring produce_plan_review_verdict\'s `with assemble_context_cache(), collect_contract_violations(), gate_config(cfg):`)' — but does not specify whether the scope wraps only run_workflow or the full function body.

In `_run_code_review_gate` (gate_dispatch.py lines 614–651): `_ex.run_workflow` is inside `with gate_source.gate_read_root(handle):` (closes at line 627); `finalize_code_review_verdict` is called at line 644, OUTSIDE that block.

The plan-review analogue (`produce_plan_review_verdict`, gate_dispatch.py line 171) wraps the entire `_ex.run_workflow` call AND the downstream drain (in `plan_review_coach`) under one `collect_contract_violations()` scope — the drain happens inside the scope. Code-review's finalize is structurally separated from the run.

ABSENCE: the plan never specifies the nesting order relative to `gate_read_root`, leaving implementers to guess; a naive 'mirror plan-review' reading puts the scope inside gate_read_root and loses the drain.
findings.finding: The plan does not resolve the ContextVar scope boundary for `collect_contract_violations()` in `_run_code_review_gate`: if the context manager wraps only `_ex.run_workflow` (inside `gate_source.gate_read_root(handle):`), its ContextVar token is reset before `finalize_code_review_verdict` is called (which is outside the `gate_read_root` block, at lines 644–651). `drain_contract_violations()` inside `finalize_code_review_verdict` would then see no active sink and silently return `[]`, making the entire contract-violation drain a no-op for code-review despite no error.
findings.location: Remediation §1 / `gate_dispatch._run_code_review_gate` (lines 614–651)
scenarios: Implementer nests `collect_contract_violations()` inside `with gate_source.gate_read_root(handle):` alongside `_ex.run_workflow`; the ContextVar is reset when gate_read_root exits; `drain_contract_violations()` in `finalize_code_review_verdict` returns [] silently — all violations are lost and `coverage['verification_contract_violations']` is never set.
A future reader auditing 'why do code-review violations not appear in coverage' has no plan text to explain the scope decision.
findings.suggested_fix: In Remediation §1, replace 'activated in gate_dispatch._run_code_review_gate' with: 'wrap the entire _run_code_review_gate body — from the handle/cfg/runner_sel setup through finalize_code_review_verdict — in `with collect_contract_violations():`, so the sink is active for the full workflow run AND the finalize drain. Do NOT nest it inside gate_source.gate_read_root(handle), which exits before finalize_code_review_verdict is called.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether `plan_review/orchestrator.py` retains a re-export shim (expand-contract) for the relocated sink symbols, or provide explicit confirmation that no test or caller imports them directly from that path — the plan must reconcile 'update call sites' with 'test suites pass unmodified'.
evidence: Remediation: 'Update plan_review/orchestrator.py's and plan_review/workflow_ops.py's existing call sites to import it from its new home'
AC3: 'Existing plan-review and code-review test suites continue to pass unmodified (the sink move is a mechanical relocation — plan-review's behavior is byte-identical)'
ABSENCE: no re-export shim, no expand-contract sequence, no rollback path specified for the import-path change
findings.finding: The Remediation directs updating `plan_review/orchestrator.py`'s and `plan_review/workflow_ops.py`'s import call sites to the new `review_kernel` home, but AC3 promises existing test suites pass 'unmodified.' The plan does not specify whether the old module location retains a re-export shim — if any test (or unidentified caller) imports the sink symbols directly from `plan_review/orchestrator.py`, the relocation silently breaks them without any stated expand-contract or rollback mechanism.
findings.location: Remediation item 1 vs AC3; `plan_review/orchestrator.py` sink relocation
scenarios: A unit test that does `from rebar.llm.plan_review.orchestrator import collect_contract_violations` fails with ImportError after the relocation — breaking the test suite the AC guarantees will pass.
A future caller added between now and merge that imports from the old path silently breaks with no migration path.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to Remediation item 1: 'Leave a re-export shim in `plan_review/orchestrator.py` (`from review_kernel.telemetry import collect_contract_violations, record_contract_violation, drain_contract_violations`) until a follow-on cleanup ticket removes it, so no existing importer breaks.' Then AC3's 'unmodified' guarantee is mechanically satisfied.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a structural proving command to the verify section (e.g., `grep -rn 'collect_contract_violations\|record_contract_violation\|drain_contract_violations' src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py src/rebar/llm/code_review/` to assert zero definitions outside `review_kernel`) or require a new test that imports the sink symbol from `review_kernel` and asserts both pipeline modules import from the same object identity.
evidence: AC2: 'code_review_decide records contract violations via the SAME shared … sink … moved into review_kernel … not a duplicate'

Verify commands: only `pytest tests/unit/code_review -q` and `pytest tests -q` — no structural check (e.g., `grep -r 'from rebar.llm.review_kernel' … code_review/workflow_ops.py`) that confirms the import provenance

A test that exercises both pipelines through the sink could pass even if two identical ContextVar definitions exist in separate modules if both are named the same and happen to work identically in isolation
findings.finding: The verify commands (all `pytest … -q`) cannot distinguish a correctly MOVED sink from a merely DUPLICATED one; the architectural requirement in AC2 ('SAME shared … sink … not a duplicate') is not provable by test execution alone unless a specific structural import-provenance test is required, but no such test is mandated by name or by a grep/import assertion in the verify section.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC2; Verify commands section
scenarios: An executor implements the sink in both orchestrator.py (unchanged) and a new review_kernel/telemetry.py for code-review, making both pipelines 'work' and all tests pass, while violating the 'single implementation' structural requirement of AC2.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Verify commands section: `grep -rn 'def collect_contract_violations\|def record_contract_violation\|def drain_contract_violations' src/rebar/llm/ | grep -v review_kernel` and assert the output is empty (non-zero exit = duplication detected). This is a one-liner structural guard that the existing pytest suite cannot provide.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand the AC's testing requirement for `decide_outcome_counts()` to explicitly enumerate tests for each of its four branches: (1) findings non-empty + raw_verifs empty → empty_outcomes=1, (2) findings present + verifications present + reshape.has_violations → recovered=1, (3) findings present + verifications present + no violations → clean=1, (4) findings empty → all zeros (or document the expected behavior).
evidence: AC says only 'cover code-review's outcome_counts/contract-violation propagation specifically' without enumerating branches

Remediation describes 4 distinct output combinations: empty_outcomes=1 (findings non-empty, raw_verifs empty), recovered=1 (reshape.has_violations), clean=1 (no violations), and always-0 unrecoverable

The `empty_outcomes` path is specifically a failure/degraded-run signal — it would only be exercised if a test supplies non-empty findings with an empty verifications list, which is not explicitly required by the AC
findings.finding: The AC testing requirement for `decide_outcome_counts()` is vague — 'new tests cover code-review's outcome_counts propagation specifically' — and does not enumerate the four distinct conditional branches of the helper. A developer could satisfy the AC by testing only the clean/happy path (findings present, verifications present, no violations → `clean=1`) while leaving the `empty_outcomes=1` branch (findings present but `raw_verifs` completely empty — a degraded/failure mode) and the `recovered=1` branch (verifications present but `reshape.has_violations`) untested. Since this is a new shared helper both pipelines import, an untested branch could silently compute wrong coverage values in production without any test catching it.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, bullet 1; Remediation section 2 (`decide_outcome_counts` helper branch logic)
scenarios: Verify step returns zero verifications for non-empty findings: `empty_outcomes` should be 1 but is 0 if branch is untested
Verify step returns malformed entries causing reshape.has_violations: `recovered` should be 1 but is 0 if branch is untested
Wrong outcome_counts silently flows into coverage without any test failure
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the AC: 'Tests for `decide_outcome_counts()` MUST cover all four branches: empty-verifications/non-empty-findings (empty_outcomes=1), has-violations (recovered=1), clean run (clean=1), and always-zero unrecoverable; at least one test per branch is required.' This makes the branches explicit and prevents a developer from satisfying the AC with only the happy path.

evidence: vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the definitive target file for the moved sink (e.g., 'use `review_kernel/telemetry.py`; create it if absent') so the executor has a single unambiguous location.
evidence: 'e.g. `review_kernel/verify.py` or a new `review_kernel/telemetry.py`' — two alternatives given, no preference stated
This matches the 'choose an appropriate X' placeholder pattern flagged by the E2 rubric
The executor cannot pick a location without guessing intent; a subsequent rename would force another import-fixup pass
findings.finding: The plan offers two candidate locations for the moved ContextVar sink ('e.g. review_kernel/verify.py or a new review_kernel/telemetry.py') without stating a preferred default, leaving a real file-placement design choice to the executor.
findings.location: Remediation, item 1: 'Move the existing ContextVar-backed sink ... into `review_kernel` (e.g. `review_kernel/verify.py` or a new `review_kernel/telemetry.py`)'
scenarios: Executor places the sink in verify.py; a reviewer or CI linter enforces a separate telemetry module convention and the PR is rejected.
Two agents working in parallel pick different locations, causing a merge conflict.
findings.suggested_fix: In Remediation item 1, replace 'e.g. `review_kernel/verify.py` or a new `review_kernel/telemetry.py`' with a single stated default such as 'a new `review_kernel/telemetry.py`' (preferred: keeps verify.py focused on logic, telemetry.py on instrumentation) and remove the alternative.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the absence-tolerance contract of the moved sink: document (and test) what `record_contract_violation` and `drain_contract_violations` do when called outside an active `collect_contract_violations()` context, and require new code-review tests to activate the context manager explicitly so propagation assertions are non-vacuous.
evidence: Plan specifies activation in `gate_dispatch._run_code_review_gate` and drain in `finalize_code_review_verdict`, but is silent on sink behavior when `collect_contract_violations()` has not been entered
T9 checklist item: 'Consumers enumerated and tolerant of absence/staleness' — tolerance of absence is not addressed

AC says new tests 'cover code-review's outcome_counts/contract-violation propagation specifically' without specifying that tests must wrap with the context manager, leaving open the possibility of vacuous passing tests
findings.finding: The plan specifies WHERE `collect_contract_violations()` is activated (gate_dispatch) and WHERE `drain_contract_violations()` is called (finalize step), but does not specify what `record_contract_violation` or `drain_contract_violations` should do when called outside an active `collect_contract_violations()` context — for example, in unit tests that exercise `code_review_decide` in isolation without the full gate_dispatch wrapper. If the moved sink raises (rather than silently no-ops) when no context is active, new tests for `code_review_decide`'s contract-violation path will require the test harness to activate the context manager, but the AC does not mandate this. If it silently no-ops, tests that omit the wrapper will pass vacuously and miss real propagation failures. Either way, the absence-tolerance contract of the moved sink is unspecified.
findings.location: Remediation step 1 / AC line 2: 'at least one test covers the moved sink from its new shared location for both pipelines'
scenarios: A unit test for `code_review_decide` that calls `record_contract_violation` without first entering `collect_contract_violations()` either raises a LookupError/AttributeError (if the ContextVar has no default) causing the test suite to fail, or silently discards the violation (if default is None/empty) causing the propagation assertion to pass vacuously — both outcomes mask real bugs in the wiring.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the sink's moved location specifying: 'If called outside an active `collect_contract_violations()` context, `record_contract_violation` is a no-op and `drain_contract_violations` returns `[]`.' Update the AC to require that new code-review contract-violation tests wrap the relevant call under `with collect_contract_violations():` so the drain assertion is causally meaningful.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `decide_outcome_counts` is worth extracting as a named `review_kernel` helper at only 2 call sites, or document in the plan why the 6-line logic is non-trivial enough to justify a shared seam over per-`*_decide` inline code with a shared comment.
evidence: Plan: 'Add a small shared helper in review_kernel — e.g. decide_outcome_counts(raw_verifs: list, findings: list, reshape: VerificationReshape) -> dict[str, int]'
Grep for `decide_outcome_counts` returns zero existing hits — the helper does not yet exist
Call sites enumerated in plan: exactly 2 (`plan_review_decide`, `code_review_decide`)
Logic described: empty_outcomes = 1 if findings non-empty and raw_verifs empty else 0; unrecoverable always 0; clean/recovered from reshape.has_violations — approximately 4-6 lines
Rule-of-Three: 2 < 3
findings.finding: `decide_outcome_counts` is a new shared `review_kernel` helper with only 2 proposed call sites (`plan_review_decide` and `code_review_decide`), falling below the Rule-of-Three bar for a justified shared abstraction. The plan's AC mandates 'no duplicated tallying logic,' which is a real deduplication motive, but the logic described (3-4 branches, ~6 lines total) is simple enough to inline in each `*_decide` function without risking silent drift — especially since `unrecoverable` is hardcoded 0 and `empty_outcomes` is a single boolean check. The plan does not affirmatively argue why a named helper is preferred over inline duplication at this scale.
findings.location: Remediation § 2 — `decide_outcome_counts` helper; AC line 1
scenarios: If a third `*_decide` consumer never materialises, this helper is a premature abstraction that adds indirection for minimal payoff.

If the tally logic is inlined, the AC ('no duplicated tallying logic') could still be satisfied by a comment pointing both impls at the same documented rule — at this trivial scale, inline + comment may be cleaner than a named seam.

findings.finding: The plan's primary remediation framing — 'Wire both plan_review_decide and code_review_decide to call the shared verify_findings()' — is architecturally incorrect: verify_findings() requires an injected run_chunk: RunChunk LLM seam and re-executes the LLM call, but both _decide ops receive verifications as already-completed raw_verifs inputs (the workflow prompt step ran previously). Directly calling verify_findings() in either decide op would issue a second LLM call. The plan's own parenthetical '(or extract/reuse its chunk-running + tallying logic)' is the only viable path, but it is relegated to a parenthetical and the primary framing is misleading.
findings.location: Remediation section; verify.py:281-363 (verify_findings signature requires run_chunk: RunChunk); code_review/workflow_ops.py:395-450 (code_review_decide receives raw_verifs as pre-completed input); plan_review/workflow_ops.py:425-455 (same pattern)

findings.finding: The plan's primary remediation verb — 'Wire both plan_review_decide and code_review_decide to call the shared verify_findings()' — is a potential golden-hammer misapplication: verify_findings() requires a RunChunk callable and drives LLM calls itself, but both decide steps are post-LLM steps that receive an already-executed flat verifications list from the workflow engine. Calling verify_findings() at the decide step would force those steps to also accept RunChunk/window_tokens/est_tokens and re-run or re-mock the LLM tier — contradicting AC4 ('no change to … verdict shape') and the workflow split-step architecture. The parenthetical hedge ('or extract/reuse its chunk-running + tallying logic') is architecturally correct for this context but is underspecified: the decide steps only have the merged flat list and cannot reconstruct chunk-level clean/recovered/empty_outcomes/unrecoverable tallies without access to per-chunk outputs that the workflow engine has already discarded.
findings.location: Remediation section: 'Wire both plan_review_decide and code_review_decide to call the shared verify_findings()'; AC1: 'outcome_counts (clean/recovered/empty_outcomes/unrecoverable) from the same shared kernel logic'

findings.finding: AC3 requires new tests only for the code-review pipeline's outcome_counts/contract-violation propagation, but plan_review_decide is also being re-wired to call verify_findings() instead of calling reshape_verifications() directly. The AC only says existing plan-review suites must 'continue to pass', which could be satisfied even if the new delegation path in plan_review_decide is never exercised by any test (e.g., existing tests that stub reshape_verifications directly would still pass while the new verify_findings call is silently wrong or ignored).
findings.location: AC3 — 'Existing plan-review and code-review test suites continue to pass; new tests cover the code-review pipeline's outcome_counts/contract-violation propagation specifically.'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC3 to: 'New tests cover both the plan-review and code-review pipelines' outcome_counts/contract-violation propagation from verify_findings(), not only code-review.'

findings.finding: The code-review pipeline has no equivalent of plan_review's `orchestrator.record_contract_violation` / `collect_contract_violations()` infrastructure, and `gate_dispatch.py`'s `_run_code_review_gate` does not establish any run-scoped violation sink. AC2 requires `code_review_decide` to 'record contract violations via the same mechanism plan_review_decide uses (or a documented equivalent)', but neither names what that equivalent is nor notes that it must be created — leaving the creation scope and the necessary gate_dispatch.py activation unspecified.
findings.location: AC2; src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py (_run_code_review_gate, line 592); src/rebar/llm/code_review/ (no orchestrator module)

findings.finding: The plan's primary mechanism — wiring `*_decide` to call `verify_findings()` — is architecturally incompatible with the workflow step boundary: both `plan_review_decide` and `code_review_decide` receive a pre-merged flat `verifications` list from the workflow runner (`_merge_chunked_outputs`), not raw per-chunk outputs, so `verify_findings()` (which owns the `run_chunk` loop and computes `outcome_counts` per chunk during execution) cannot be called from the decide step. The per-chunk signals `empty_outcomes` (chunk returned empty despite input) and `unrecoverable` (StructuredOutputError per chunk) are irrecoverably lost after the workflow merges chunk outputs.
findings.location: Remediation section + `src/rebar/llm/code_review/workflow_ops.py:395–450` (`code_review_decide`) and `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/workflow_ops.py:425–496` (`plan_review_decide`) + `src/rebar/llm/workflow/runs.py:103–120` (`_merge_chunked_outputs`)
findings.suggested_fix: Move outcome_counts computation to `*_verify_inputs` (or a new `*_verify_tally` step that wraps the verify prompt step's per-chunk outputs before merging). Emit `outcome_counts` and `contract_violations` as separate named outputs of that step, then thread them into `*_coach` (for coverage recording) rather than `*_decide`. Alternatively, scope AC1 to 'clean/recovered from reshape' only (achievable in `*_decide` from the flat list) and explicitly accept that `empty_outcomes`/`unrecoverable` require a later phase change.

findings.finding: AC2 references a 'documented equivalent' mechanism for code_review_decide to record contract violations, but the plan never defines what that equivalent is or where the violation data flows in the code-review pipeline. Unlike plan-review — which has a ContextVar-backed collect_contract_violations context manager activated in gate_dispatch, a record_contract_violation call in plan_review_decide, and a drain_contract_violations call writing to verdict coverage — the code-review pipeline has no analogous sink, no context manager in _run_code_review_gate, and no drain path in finalize_code_review_verdict. An executor reading only the plan cannot determine how to propagate outcome_counts or contract violations out of code_review_decide into the verdict coverage.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, AC2: 'code_review_decide records contract violations via the same mechanism plan_review_decide uses (or a documented equivalent)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to AC2 such as: 'The documented equivalent is: `code_review_decide` writes `outcome_counts` and `contract_violations` as output keys on its step result dict; `finalize_code_review_verdict` (or `code_review_coach`) reads them and writes `coverage["verification_contract_violations"]` and `coverage["outcome_counts"]` when non-empty — mirroring the plan-review `drain_contract_violations()` → `coverage` write without requiring a new ContextVar sink.'

findings.finding: AC1 uses 'surface outcome_counts' without specifying the observable form — it is unspecified whether the counts must appear in a structured return value, a log record, an orchestrator telemetry call, or some other sink — making the criterion subjective to evaluate.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'both surface outcome_counts … from the same shared kernel logic' with 'both pipelines return outcome_counts as a structured field from the decide function AND record it via [named orchestrator/telemetry call], using the same verify_findings() invocation path.'

findings.finding: The Remediation section presents two design choices without stating a preference: 'call the shared verify_findings() OR extract/reuse its chunk-running + tallying logic', leaving a real architectural fork (monolithic call vs. extracted helper) to the executor.
findings.location: Remediation section
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '(or extract/reuse its chunk-running + tallying logic)' with '(direct delegation to verify_findings() is preferred; extraction is permitted only when the pipeline's existing signature makes direct call infeasible, and must be documented in a code comment).'

findings.finding: The Remediation permits 'extract/reuse its chunk-running + tallying logic' as an alternative to calling verify_findings() directly, but AC1 requires outcome_counts from 'the same shared kernel logic' — an implementer who duplicates the tallying logic (rather than calling the shared function) would satisfy the Remediation guidance but potentially violate AC1's 'same shared kernel logic' wording, sending implementers in two directions.
findings.location: Remediation paragraph vs. AC1
findings.suggested_fix: Remove '(or extract/reuse its chunk-running + tallying logic)' from the Remediation and keep the mandate as 'call the shared verify_findings()'; this aligns with AC1's 'same shared kernel logic' and the ticket's stated goal of uniform telemetry.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Retitle or add a one-line clarifying note at the top distinguishing `verify_findings()` (not wired — stays reserved) from the new `decide_outcome_counts()` helper that IS the deliverable, so the title and body are unambiguously aligned.
evidence: Title: 'Wire shared verify_findings outcome_counts telemetry into plan_review and code_review consumer pipelines'
Finding section: 'Two concrete, single-path changes (no run_chunk/verify_findings() involvement — that kernel entry point stays reserved for callers that own the procedural chunk loop)'
AC-1: 'computed by ONE shared review_kernel helper' — the helper is decide_outcome_counts, not verify_findings()'
The plan's own Finding section says neither pipeline CAN call verify_findings() as-is
findings.finding: The plan's title promises to 'Wire shared verify_findings outcome_counts telemetry' — implying the existing `verify_findings()` function is being wired into the pipelines — but the body explicitly states `verify_findings()` is NOT called by either pipeline and will NOT be called by this ticket. Instead, the plan introduces a NEW helper (`decide_outcome_counts`) that produces a coarser, always-`unrecoverable=0` tally at a different architectural boundary. A reader following the title would expect `verify_findings()` to become the shared entry point, but the actual deliverable is a boundary-adapted substitute.
findings.location: Title vs. Remediation section, item 2 / Acceptance Criteria AC-1
scenarios: An implementer reading the title would look for a way to invoke verify_findings() from the pipeline and be confused when the plan says not to; the mismatch between the named function in the title and the actual deliverable (a new helper) could cause the agent to build the wrong thing.
findings.suggested_fix: Retitle to: 'Wire shared outcome_counts telemetry and contract-violation sink into plan_review and code_review consumer pipelines' (dropping the verify_findings reference that implies the existing function is being called).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief note to `AGENTS.md` (or equivalent design doc) documenting that `review_kernel` owns the canonical contract-violation sink and `decide_outcome_counts` helper, so future pipeline authors import from there rather than re-implementing.
evidence: Move the existing ContextVar-backed sink ... into `review_kernel` (e.g. `review_kernel/verify.py` or a new `review_kernel/telemetry.py`) as the ONE implementation both pipelines import
No mention of AGENTS.md, ADR, or design-doc update anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The plan establishes a new shared architectural contract — `review_kernel` now owns the canonical contract-violation sink and outcome-counts telemetry used by both consumer pipelines — but mentions no update to AGENTS.md, an ADR, or any design doc to capture this decision. Future contributors adding a third consumer may not know the sink lives in `review_kernel` rather than each pipeline's own orchestrator.
findings.location: Remediation section (step 1) and Acceptance Criteria AC-2
scenarios: A developer adding a third pipeline (e.g. a non-workflow consumer) looks for the sink in orchestrator.py (where it lived before) and re-duplicates it instead of importing from review_kernel.
A reviewer auditing review_kernel's public surface doesn't know the ContextVar sink is now a shared contract and removes or renames it.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a section to `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/AGENTS.md` (or update the existing one) noting: 'This module owns the shared contract-violation ContextVar sink (`collect_contract_violations`/`record_contract_violation`/`drain_contract_violations`) and `decide_outcome_counts` helper. All consumer pipelines (plan_review, code_review, and future additions) must import these from here — do not re-implement per-pipeline.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
